description: Actively ruling out diagnoses one by one using clinical evidence, test results, and contradicting findings rather than just picking the most obvious answer.

clues.text: Chest X-ray shows no mediastinal widening.
Lung ultrasound shows normal lung sliding bilaterally.
D-dimer is within normal limits.
Pain is not relieved by antacids and is associated with diaphoresis.

drillLibrary.commonMistakes: Eliminating MI prematurely due to a single normal EKG (which can be normal early).
drillLibrary.teachingPoint: Systematic elimination requires high-sensitivity tests to rule out life-threatening conditions.

drillLibrary.scenario: A 72-year-old female with sudden right-sided weakness.

clues.text: Fingerstick glucose is 110 mg/dL.
No history of seizures or post-ictal state.
Non-contrast CT head shows no intracranial bleeding.
Patient has no history of headaches and symptoms are purely focal motor.

drillLibrary.commonMistakes: Assuming ischemic stroke before ruling out hypoglycemia or hemorrhage.
drillLibrary.teachingPoint: CT head is the gold standard for eliminating hemorrhage, while glucose is the first step to eliminate metabolic mimics.

clues.text: Urine pregnancy test is negative.
Urinalysis shows no hematuria or crystals.
Pelvic ultrasound shows normal arterial and venous flow to both ovaries.
Pain is migratory and associated with anorexia and fever.

drillLibrary.commonMistakes: Eliminating ectopic pregnancy without a pregnancy test.
drillLibrary.teachingPoint: A negative pregnancy test is the most powerful tool to logically eliminate ectopic pregnancy in females of childbearing age.

clues.text: Lung auscultation is clear with no wheezing.
Chest X-ray shows no focal infiltrates or consolidations.
Chest X-ray shows no pleural line or lung collapse.
EKG shows sinus tachycardia but no ST changes; Troponin is negative.

drillLibrary.commonMistakes: Eliminating PE based on a normal EKG or CXR (which are often normal in PE).
drillLibrary.teachingPoint: PE is often a 'diagnosis of exclusion' on basic imaging (normal CXR with severe dyspnea).

drillLibrary.scenario: An 80-year-old female with cough and fever.

clues.text: BNP is 80 pg/mL (normal < 100).
D-dimer is negative.
Chest X-ray shows a dense consolidation in the RLL.
Symptoms are acute (3 days) with high fever and leukocytosis.

drillLibrary.commonMistakes: Eliminating pneumonia in the elderly because they lack a high fever (they often don't mount one).
drillLibrary.teachingPoint: BNP and D-dimer are powerful tools to eliminate HF and PE when pneumonia is suspected.

drillLibrary.scenario: A 20-year-old male with headache and fever.

clues.text: CT head shows no blood in the subarachnoid space.
CT head with contrast shows no ring-enhancing lesions.
Patient is alert and oriented with no focal neurological deficits or seizures.
Patient has high fever and significant nuchal rigidity.

drillLibrary.commonMistakes: Eliminating meningitis before encephalitis (which requires altered mental status).
drillLibrary.teachingPoint: Encephalitis is eliminated by the absence of altered mental status or focal brain dysfunction.

clues.text: Leg is not erythematous or warm; no fever.
Swelling is unilateral and acute (2 days).
Ultrasound of the popliteal fossa shows no fluid collection.

drillLibrary.commonMistakes: Eliminating DVT because the leg isn't 'red enough' (cellulitis is the mimic).
drillLibrary.teachingPoint: DVT is eliminated by a negative compression ultrasound, the gold standard.

drillLibrary.scenario: A 70-year-old male with hypotension and fever.

clues.text: No history of allergen exposure; no hives or wheezing.
No history of trauma; skin is warm and flushed.
Echocardiogram shows normal EF and no wall motion abnormalities.
Patient has received 3L of fluids with minimal improvement in MAP; IVC is full on ultrasound.

drillLibrary.commonMistakes: Eliminating sepsis in favor of hypovolemia without checking fluid responsiveness.
drillLibrary.teachingPoint: Shock differentiation requires evaluating the heart (Echo) and fluid status (IVC/Fluid challenge).

drillLibrary.scenario: A 15-year-old with abdominal pain and vomiting.

clues.text: Serum lipase is normal.
Abdominal ultrasound shows a compressible appendix of normal diameter.
Toxicology screen is negative.
Patient has Kussmaul breathing and a blood glucose of 450 mg/dL.

drillLibrary.commonMistakes: Eliminating DKA because the patient has 'surgical' abdominal pain.
drillLibrary.teachingPoint: DKA can mimic a surgical abdomen; glucose and ketones are the keys to elimination.

drillLibrary.scenario: A 25-year-old with sudden wheezing and hives.

clues.text: Symptoms are multi-system (skin + respiratory).
Patient is hypotensive (BP 80/50) and has visible angioedema.

drillLibrary.commonMistakes: Eliminating anaphylaxis in favor of asthma when hives are present.
drillLibrary.teachingPoint: Multi-system involvement (skin + another system) is the 'rule-in' for anaphylaxis and 'rule-out' for isolated asthma.

clues.text: Liver ultrasound shows no cirrhosis; albumin is normal.
D-dimer is negative.
Chest X-ray shows no focal infiltrates but has Kerley B lines.
Patient has JVD and an S3 gallop; no wheezing.

drillLibrary.commonMistakes: Eliminating HF because the patient has a history of smoking (assuming COPD).
drillLibrary.teachingPoint: JVD and S3 are highly specific signs that eliminate non-cardiac causes of dyspnea.

clues.text: No flank pain or CVA tenderness; afebrile.
No hematuria; CT KUB shows no stones.
Pelvic exam shows no discharge or cervical motion tenderness.

drillLibrary.commonMistakes: Eliminating UTI in favor of vaginitis without a pelvic exam.
drillLibrary.teachingPoint: The absence of systemic signs (fever/flank pain) eliminates pyelonephritis, leaving cystitis as the primary diagnosis.

evaluationRubric.correct: Successfully eliminated all incorrect diagnoses using the provided clues; correct diagnosis remains.
evaluationRubric.partiallyCorrect: Eliminated most incorrect diagnoses but kept one or two wrong ones alongside the correct one.
evaluationRubric.wrong: Eliminated the correct diagnosis or failed to eliminate obvious distractors.

commonErrors.error: Premature elimination (ruling out the correct answer)

progressionStages.name: Guided Recognition
progressionStages.scaffolding: Each clue explicitly states which diagnosis it eliminates (e.g., 'This rules out X').

progressionStages.name: Unguided Application
progressionStages.scaffolding: Clues are provided without explanation; user must decide what they eliminate.
progressionStages.interaction: Select clue -> Select diagnosis to eliminate.

progressionStages.name: Pressured Performance
progressionStages.scaffolding: Clues are released on a timer (every 10 seconds).
progressionStages.interaction: Eliminate before the next clue arrives.

progressionStages.name: Expert Calibration
progressionStages.scaffolding: Includes 'red herring' clues that don't eliminate anything.
progressionStages.interaction: Eliminate + Rate confidence in each elimination.

adaptiveRules.prioritization: Prioritize cases where the correct diagnosis was eliminated prematurely.

masteryDefinition.consistency: Zero premature eliminations of the correct diagnosis in Stage 3
